meta.description: Calibration anchors from professional consultant housing needs assessments, for comparing this repo's computed HNA figures against industry practice. Extracted and verified from the source PDFs on 2026-07-04.
meta.concept_warning: Repo and consultant figures rarely measure the identical concept. Every anchor carries a 'concept' and 'caveat' field; ratios are calibration signals, not error measurements.

report.title: La Plata County Housing Needs Assessment
report.author: Root Policy Research
report.data_vintages: ACS 2022 5-yr, HUD 2024 income limits, SDO projections (pre-2023-revision vintage)

anchors.concept: Cumulative rental shortage below 50% AMI: renter households vs renter units priced affordable (2,715 renters vs 1,752 units)
anchors.caveat: Repo demand side is ALL-tenure households (B19001) vs renter-only supply; Root uses renter households only. Repo runs ~4.6x high pre-fix; a tenure fix is in flight (2026-07).

anchors.concept: Rental shortage in the 0-30% AMI tier (1,446 renters vs 795 units)
anchors.caveat: Same tenure-mixing caveat as rental_gap_lte50_ami. Repo ~3.9x high pre-fix.

anchors.concept: New units needed 2024-2029 to keep up with SDO household growth, 5% rental / 2% ownership vacancy allowances, 71% ownership split
anchors.caveat: Root used a pre-revision SDO vintage (2030 pop 61,656); current vintage-2023 SDO forecast is 59,471 and the repo's components-of-change trajectory is within -1.9% of it. Repo's lower number reflects the CURRENT official forecast, not an understatement.

report.title: Pueblo Housing Assessment and Strategy
report.author: Gruen Gruen + Associates
report.data_vintages: ACS 2019, CDLE 2020-2030 employment projections, DOLA SYA

anchors.caveat: GG+A layered 2021-era job growth (47% from Springs/Denver spillover), 1:1 senior household demand, and replacement need. Current DOLA has Pueblo County population peaking ~2035. Repo's growth-only figure is structurally smaller; replacement component added to repo 2026-07.

report.title: Pueblo Housing Assessment and Strategy
report.author: Gruen Gruen + Associates
report.data_vintages: ACS 2019, +25% owner-value appreciation adjustment to early 2021

anchors.concept: City rental units deficit below $375/mo gross rent (supply 2,139 vs 4,776 households able to afford only that)
anchors.caveat: Thresholds differ materially: GG+A's cut is <$375/mo (2019 incomes); repo's 30% AMI threshold rent is ~$659 (HUD FY2025). Repo value should exceed GG+A's. Repo's figure also tracks CHAS renter cost-burden counts (~8.8k) almost 1:1.

anchors.concept: Existing need: MLS listing shortfall vs balanced-market months-of-supply (36-61 for-sale + 11-18 rental)
anchors.caveat: NOT the same concept: Ayres measures market tightness (listing inventory), not affordability mismatch. Ayres publishes no cost-burden analysis at all. Expect repo > Ayres.

Repo has NO place-level projections (places carry projectionMode county_scaled); parent Weld County incremental 2030 is 15,304 countywide. Anchor recorded for when place-level downscaling lands (permit-share weighting proposed 2026-07).

report.title: Alamosa Housing Action Plan (needs figure from companion Alamosa Housing Needs Assessment 2021)
report.author: Williford LLC / WSW Consulting
report.data_vintages: 2021 HUD income limits; underlying HNA published Jan 2021

anchors.concept: City 5-year need combining current gaps + job growth (catch-up and keep-up not split); adopted production goal 350 by 2026
anchors.caveat: Consultant number bundles growth aspiration; DOLA has Alamosa County near-flat (county incremental 49 by 2030). Repo's ~569 deep-affordability gap happens to sit inside the consultant's total range but measures a different thing.

report.title: Housing Needs Assessment and Affordable Housing Strategy
report.author: czbLLC

anchors.concept: czbLLC explicitly declined to publish a unit-count gap ('a subjective, not objective, exercise', p.16); report quantifies per-unit dollar feasibility gaps instead
anchors.caveat: No consultant number exists to compare. Repo's 30% AMI gap (~559) is consistent with czb's qualitative finding that ~1% of Erie stock is rentals affordable below $50K income. czb's cost-burden count (~2,200 HH) matches repo CHAS cb30 total (2,224) within 1%.
